title: Turb spheres w/ smoothed edges
description:
New features & bug-fixes for turb-sphere.py initial condition generator. This PR is also a test run of new code review process!

**Summary of changes:**

* New flag `--rho-smooth` which applies a tanh kernel to sphere edges, to help stabilize spheres w/ sharp density contrasts
* New flag `--seed` to set numpy random seed
* Bugfix to `--no_data`
* Change `--with_plots` to `--no_plots`, so the flag actually does something \(previously, had no effect\)
* Miscellaneous changes: replace `import yt` with hard-coded constants, alter the setting of exterior ISM density, subtle change to radial profile sampling

NOTE: `--rho_smooth` is not a panacea, and can give sharp discontinuities if density goes below n ~ 0.1 \(transition from WIM to HIM\). User should tweak the external ISM properties to help w/ pressure matching.

**Testing:**

* \[x\] create spheres on two different computers with same random seed; output data files are \(effectively\) identical

    * NOTE: in 128^3 array, three cells were not identical due to rounding.
    
* \[x\] create sphere w/ smoothed dens profile, plots look reasonable
* \[x\] use -np flag, outputs are as expected
* \[x\] use -nd flag, outputs are as expected

Default behavior: create a sphere file \+ plots

`./turb-sphere.py --mass 1e8 --radius 100 --box_side 150 -v 0.2 -n 0.001 --Ts_from_cool_curve --seed 99 --filename asdf`

Create sphere file, but no plots

`./turb-sphere.py --mass 1e8 --radius 100 --box_side 150 -v 0.2 -n 0.001 --Ts_from_cool_curve --seed 99 --filename asdf -np`

Create plots, but no sphere file

`./turb-sphere.py --mass 1e8 --radius 100 --box_side 150 -v 0.2 -n 0.001 --Ts_from_cool_curve --seed 99 --filename asdf -nd`

Create a sphere file w/ smoothed edge

`./turb-sphere.py --mass 1e8 --radius 100 --box_side 150 -v 0.2 -n 0.001 --Ts_from_cool_curve --seed 99 --rho_match --filename asdf`

title: N-body run bugfixes+enhancements
description:
Major changes:

* Vectorize make\_stars\_from\_sinks2
* Vectorize add\_particles\_to\_grav
* Bugfix: sync grav/hydro time at first star

    Else first grav dt is MUCH too big


* Restock sinks when all\_masses\[...\] is low

Minor changes:

* Remove sleep statements
* Initial cluster: set oldmass in hydro

    Else, wrong/zero stellar ZAMS mass will break stellar evol code



Testing:

* compared code pre/post-vectorize, verified code runs with noticeable speedup

    * this is a significant cost for low-resolution runs adding ~1000s of stars at a time, or for restarts from runs with ~1000s or 10,000s of stars.
    
* checked first grav dt in STDOUT, now reports correctly
* run with arbitrarily low sink queue mass \(say 10 Msun vs 10000 Msun\) triggers sink restocking
* setting “oldmass” in hydro allows simulation with initial cluster to run \(breaks otherwise\)

title: Merging in Refactor into Master
description:
Hey Everyone,

Figured I would set up this merge for `refactor` into `master` as everyone is currently using it. 

~ Joe G.

CHANGELOG ------------------------

* Refactor bridge\_multiples from June/July
* Fix up a link \(for code reading\)
* Reading copies of bridge code, Dec2018-Mar2019
* Minimal WORKING refactor v2, October 9-11
* Re-order file IO logic
* Tidy main loop logic and stdout
* Rm some params, intermediate vars
* Parameter \+ initial-condition handling
* Cleanup, naming
* Get num workers from slurm script
* Add example user file one star
* Re-add stellar evolution

    Code runs, not regression tested


* Minor SE code cleanup
* Remove star age tracking

    Always recompute star age from hydro


* More fixes to star particle creation
* Flag to use Hermite instead of ph4
* Misc fixes, cleanup
* Sync prtl after stellar evol

    Forgot to port this over. Mass was not getting updated properly.


* Restock sinks when all\_masses\[...\] is low
* Cleanup: remove magic bridge dt number
* Multiples evolution hooks

    Everything but particle removal tentatively works, not tested


* Multiples: misc cleanup, bugfixes

    Some minor improvements/bugfixes are not related to multiples


* Execute via torch\_user.py instead of torch.py
* mv torch.py -> torch\_mainloop.py
* Multiples: particle remove
* Merge prtl remove methods for mult/non-mult
* New prtl remove: kill tree if any leaf past bndbox

    More robust particle remove algorithm for stars outside bndbox that are in multiple-star systems. This addresses edge case where multiple star system has COM within bndbox but >=1 star \(leaf\) outside bndbox.


* Reorder loop. Simpler logic, cut 1x Poisson solve

    Combine the first/second bridge kicks into one. Stars form immediately after the hydro evolve step. New "loop state" structure so that merged leapfrog kicks take the correct time step.


* Redirect FLASH stdout/stderr to file

    Add flash nstep to torch stdout to help w/ log cross-matching


* Async evolve for grav and hydro!
* Remove old refactor junk

    Notes and old reading copies of code from March,June,July 2019...


* Remove not-needed example user file
* mv refactored code to "official" dirs
* Cleanup refactor for beta testing
* Bugfix: newborn stars r unif distrib

    Bug in vectorization code: in a given timestep, all stars spawned by sink were placed at same radius \(wrt sink\). Fixed so each star placed at radius between 0 to sink\_rad.


* Gaussian distr for new star velocities
* Bugfix: ph4 "user specified ID ... already in use"

    Need to provide "id" attribute for AMUSE particle set, as was done in bridge\_multiples.py before.


* Add example fractal/plummer cluster ICs

    Port code from bridge\_multiples.py


* Pin bridge dt to FLASH dtinit, etc. params

    Allow bridge dt to be constrained by FLASH runtime parameters: dtinit, tstep\_change\_factor, dtmin, dtmax Previously, bridge dt was pinned to FLASH dr\_dtAdvect which did NOT account for these params.


* Cleaner bridge step stdout
* Fix up cluster IC code
* Remove unneeded comment
* Reorder loop to sync star&gas pos,vel output

    Revert some of the restructuring from commit 70fc834bb85d19, but try to keep time savings.


* if sink not near cold gas, don't make stars

    sink\_cs is NaN because cold gas properties are not correctly measured in Particles\_sinkCreateAccrete\(...\)


* SN ejecta mass ceiling = 15 MSun

    Fixed assert < 10 Msun, which was my unwarranted change wrt Josh's bridge\_multiples.py.


* Add example user SN setup
* Add comments for some torch\_user.py params
* Don't zero out star prtl mass after SN
* rm incorrect comment
* Mute benign exp overflow warning in stellar evol
* Cosmetic cleanup Planck func eqns and args
* Zero out feedback if restart w/feedback off

    In stellar evol, always update the feedback properties in FLASH, even if feedback is not enabled \(in which case properties are set to zero\). This ensures that, for the case:



    ```
    (run w/ feedback) -> restart -> (turn off feedback)
    ```

    the feedback properties get properly zeroed out, whereas before they would remain active for already-born stars.


* Stars restart w/correct stellar type, not ZAMS

    Prevously assumed ZAMS \(SeBa type 1\) upon restart, allowing stellar evolution code to set correctly at first restarted bridge loop. But, post-SN remnants were going SN again because code check for SN triggers upon a change in stellar type. Fix here is to reinitialize stars with correct post-SN type immediately, before the restarted bridge loop.


* Fix up prtl pointer in queue\_stars if no sinks

    Forgot to replace prtl at star prtl. Should be benign/harmless because if no sinks, no stars; and, make\_stars\_from\_sinks\(...\) should catch for us and leave pointer in correct state anyways. But good to be consistent about resetting pointer.


* Add mm-dd-YYYY to torch timestamp
* Restore FLASH particles\_sort upon restart

    In bridge\_multiples.py, but accidentally dropped in refactor port


* if not evolve\_async, don't redirect FLASH stdout
* modified print statements for python3 compatibility
* added binary flag to rnd state and star mass list pickle openings
* updated torch\_state.py to match functions in multiples.py
* Added overwrite parameter; passed param to torch\_mainloop and torch\_state; added file removal behavior to amuse write portion of state.output\(\)
* Final touch up for overwrite parameter behavior
* turned off overwrite
* removed python path file removal. passing overwrite flag to AMUSE kwarg
* Use AMUSE multiples; comment/whitespace cleanup
* consistent from future import print\_function
* rm special case logic for ph4 w/zero stars

    because ph4 can do evolve\_model without stars now, see https://github.com/amusecode/amuse/pull/564


* Edit to 'overwrite' parameter comment to make effect more clear.
* Cleanup stdout
* added B-field face value assignment
* updated comments
* embedded Bfield face assignment in #if NFACE\_VAR check to diversify split/unsplit solver usage
* introduced MAG\_FACE\_VAR index to face\{x,y,z\}Data Bfield block face assignment to diversify future face-defined data options
* fixed endif loops
* last fix to Sim init. Added sim\_killdivb to Cube Config
* syntax error fix in Cube Config
* added parameter get call in Sim\_init
* removed mag face var indexing in block pointer
* added sim\_killdivb to simulation data
* Bfield fix changes to SNSBBox
* correction to killdivb parameter get
* Bfield fix applied to EnergyInjection
* Bfield fix for StratBox
* Added factor of pi to photoelectric photon flux from solid angle integration of Planck function.
* Changed .iteritems\(\) dictionary attr to .items\(\) for Python3 functionality.
* added cartesius module environnment reference.
* Changed default IMF sample bins to 100 from 10.
* Included google group link to IMF sampling conversation
* torch\_mainloop.py - turned off multiples tidal perturbation diagnostic output. Edited online with Bitbucket

‌
author: Aaron Tran

title: New default FLASH params from Josh's newest runs
description:
From Josh's 1e3 Msun run plt/chk files \(Jan 2019\) and Josh's thesis Appendix D.  Some changes from March 2019 on are preserved \(e.g., `dust_gas_ratio = 0.01` instead of `0.02`\), so this is not an exact duplicate of Josh’s as-run parameters.

@{557058:4f379d78-cb59-4580-ba3a-1489d19e4a30} would you be able to comment on why `mg_maxResidualNorm=0.01` was raised from `1e-6`?

Two particular parameters should help stabilize simulations against hot zone \(T > 1e9 K\) crashes:

* `useHybridOrder=.true.` “\[applies\] RH jump condition to check monotonicity”, AND if a violation is found, drops back to a Godunov first-order reconstruction scheme along the non-monotonic coordinate-axis direction.  The code in `hy_uhd_checkRHjumpCond.F90` shows that…

    * this check operates in all cells with supersonic velocity.
    * this check compares density at cell-centers and face-centered reconstructed values; _no checks on pressure, velocity, B fields_
    * because this check is so simple, _it also operates upon contact discontinuities and steep rarefaction fans_… e.g. if you are supersonically advecting a cube of gas across vacuum, the edges will diffuse a lot, but it will at least not crash.  See Figure 3 of [https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2015MNRAS.450...53H/abstract](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2015MNRAS.450...53H/abstract)
    
* `hy_cflFallbackFactor=0.9` lowers the de facto global cfl from input `cfl=0.8`to `hy_cflFallbackFactor/NDIM = 0.30`. 

I’ll merge this into master by Weds Nov 27.  Justification for lack of detailed testing is:

* Since Josh chose and used these parameters for a production run, following a visit to the FLASH center, these evidently work.
* I compared these vs. old params, ceteris paribus, on very low resolution test problem \(the default parameters with lrefine=2\).  Where old params failed with hot zone crash, new params successfully reached tmax=2 Myr.

‌
author: Aaron Tran

title: Make EnergyInjection flash.par work better w/torch
description:
Problem: EnergyInjection with its included flash.par breaks in Torch, when spawning a single star in hydro, and also comes with a host of heating/cooling errors.

Sabrina reports error:

> amuse.support.exceptions.AmuseException: <-1> is not a valid value for unit<stellar\_type>

and output from `debug_se=True` \(in `bridge_multiples.py`\) showing an invalid stellar mass of zero:

> Doing stellar evolution.  
> min star age = 10000000000.0 s  
> this star initial mass before seba = 0.0 MSun  
> this star mass before seba = 0.0 MSun  
> this star type before seba = Main Sequence star 1  
> this star age = 0.000316887646218  
> elapsed time: 3.175974 ms  
> Traceback \(most recent call last\):  
> File "bridge\_multiples.py", line 3375, in <module>

### CHANGES:

Update EnergyInjection flash parameter files to work more correctly with Torch: need `useSinkParticles = T` and `tradmin > 0`.

Set default heat/cool integration method `he_int_method = "Implicit"` and  
document this parameter.

Document heat/cool params: `tradmin, tradmax, dradmin, dradmax`.

### Test procedure:

1. Setup FLASH with

    `./setup EnergyInjection -a -3d +amuseUSM +amuseMG +rayPE +HandCnew +amuseSinksAndStars +amuseWind +enerinj +supportPPMupwind +pm4dev -maxblocks=300 +cube16`


2. Set up test directory with current `bridge_multiples.py` and  
  `EnergyInjection/flash.par`.

    `mkdir test.20191220.sabrina-einj-fix ; cd test.20191220.sabrina-einj-fix ; cp $FLASH_DIR/object/flash.par . ; cp $TORCH_DIR/bridge_multiples.py . ; mkdir data`


3. Edit `flash.par` to set `lrefine_min=2`, `lrefine_max=2`.  
  Also manually add parameters

    `basenm = "einj_" output_directory= "./data"`



    in order to work with `bridge_multiples.py`.


4. Edit `bridge_multiples.py` to set `start_with_star = True` \(line 2759\).
5. Start simulation in interactive shell: `mpiexec -n 1 python bridge_multiples.py |& tee out.log`

### Test results:

* \[x\] `tradmin, tradmax, dradmin, dradmax` documentation appears in `object/setup_params`
* \[x\] test problem completes multiple hydro evolution steps, forms massive star that deposits wind \(we see stdout from `inject_direct.F90`\).

The test directory with logs/results is `test.20191220.sabrina-einj-fix` on Columbia's  
habanero cluster.

title: EnergyInjection cleanup unused code
description:
Remove old pieces of code relating to adding single particle.  Goal is to clean source code, make it more compact and readable.  Removing vestigial parameters should prevent user confusion \(e.g., setting sim\_Neph and expecting it to do something\).  The AMUSE interface for adding particles is easier to use and so preferred.

Main loss is some heritage code, for how to add particles via FLASH.  How would user re-construct this?  Answer: look at AMUSE interface code, and follow the trail of:

1. bridge\_multiples.py or torch\_user.py --- hydro.add\_particles\(…\)
2. src/amuse/interface.F90 --- Particles\_addNew\(…\)
3. $FLASH\_DIR/object/Particles\_addNew.F90 –- how to edit the particles array

Tested before and after using same flash.par and bridge\_multiples.py ; output behavior is identical.

    Previously, local variable update did not change global namespace variable, so tags\_keys got out of sync wrt hydro, ph4, amuse prtl set, multiples.


* Add Claude to contributors

The change mimics the strategy of "add\_particles\_to\_grav" in having the caller explicitly update tags\_keys, rather than declaring "global tags\_keys" and allowing the subroutine to have a [side effect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Side_effect_(computer_science)) \(though, it has plenty of side effects already, so global approach could be more consistent from that perspective\).

        z = 0.0 | units.cm,

title: Feedback fixes
description:
**Changes**

* Mute benign exp overflow warning in star’s radiation feedback calculation

    * exponential in the Planck function was overflowing to \+inf at small wavelengths and small temperatures. This is acceptable for all reasonable values of wavelength and temperature that we would use for stellar feedback \(say, wavelength > 1e-50 cm and T between 1e2 – 1e6 K\), as within this range there is no risk of numerical error from inf/inf division
    * Fixes [https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/6/overflow-warning-in-stellar-luminosity](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/6/overflow-warning-in-stellar-luminosity)
    

‌

* Fix SN inject block finding algorithm

    Old version failed on some cases, e.g. when injection is near but slightly offset from a block corner \(see Josh's notes in inject\_direct.F90\). New version is an exact overlap calculation.



    * Partially fixes [https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/4/many-sne-fail-to-deposit-energy-in-very](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/4/many-sne-fail-to-deposit-energy-in-very)
    * Fixes [https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/16/incorrect-block-distance-criterion-in-sn](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/16/incorrect-block-distance-criterion-in-sn)
    

‌

* SN deposit, initialize bgDens=0 to avoid NaN

    Without init, bgDens=NaN often prevents SN from depositing energy. The exact error behavior depends on how Fortran handles of un-initialized values, so failure may or may not occur depending on your compiler/compiler flags.



    * Partially fixes [https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/4/many-sne-fail-to-deposit-energy-in-very](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/4/many-sne-fail-to-deposit-energy-in-very)
    

‌

* SN deposit: more distinct/correct STDOUT messages

    * Previously, some things were still labeled “wind” or “inject\_direct” when this is just SN feedback.
    

**Testing**

* SN with SE test \(using default turbsph, see torch\_user.py\) at origin \(0,0,0\): worked before and works now on habanero cluster.
* SN with SE test \(using default turbsph, see torch\_user.py\) with star displaced slightly to \(\+2.7e18, \+3.7e18, \+4.7e18\): failed before, works now on habanero cluster.
* SNe in a low-res, “live” run: failed before, failed with new \{inject block finding\} algorithm, works when bgDens initialized explicitly to 0.0 \(previously caused bgDens to be NaN\). Tested on Cartesius cluster.
* Stellar evol overflow warning:

    * explicitly checked before/after fix: overflow warnings no longer seen.
    
        * Before: can generate warnings using same “SN with SE” test \(see torch\_user.py\) on Cartesius
        
    * regression: ionizing stellar feedback looks unchanged, “turbsph.dat” files agree to machine precision for at least several timesteps
    

‌

title: Read and write random stream state for feedback (winds, radiation) in restarts
description:
## Summary of changes

* Restore RNG state read/write for radiation transfer’s ray rotation
* Wind `perturb_velocity` feature: changed to use same RNG stream as rad transfer

    * Previously, used Fortran intrinsics `random_seed()` and `random_number()`
    
* RNG state files are named like `data/turbsph_txt_RNG_state_0000`. If a restart lacks RNG state file, Flash will revert to default random seed and warn to STDOUT and Flash log file.

    * Previously, file was called `RNG_state` and overwritten at each checkpoint
    
* Write RNG state file as plaintext, not binary, for cross-arch compatibility.

    * Breaks backwards compatibility with old RNG state files \(e.g., from Juan Ibanez-Mejia’s stratified box runs\).
    
* Modify `bridge_multiples.py` restart behavior to NOT recalculate star-gas grav accel for first kick upon restart, in order to match normal \(non-restart\) loop behavior. We need this consistency to get bitwise reproducible restarts from _different_ checkpoints\).

Fixes issue originally reported by Sean and Steve in: [https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/14/refactor-randomness-is-not-nailed-down](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/14/refactor-randomness-is-not-nailed-down)

See also some general notes on randomness in Torch at: [https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/wiki/Controlling%20randomness](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/wiki/Controlling%20randomness)

## Testing

To get consistent timestepping at restart, one has to:

* In `bridge_multiples.py`, disable bridge-loop control of timestepping. That requires:

    * Always set `dt=min(1.5*hy_dt,tmax-t)` and don’t use bridge `dtinit` or `dt_old`.
    * Modify `interface.F90:get_timestep()` to return `dr_dt` instead of `dr_dtAdvect`; already done in branch:refactor, but not branch:master.
    
* Ensure cfl at restart matches \(so, generally must force `cfl=0.3` to match limit imposed by hybrid order and shock detection in Hydro unit\)
* Ensure dt at restart matches; must set `rt_useRadTransDt=F` , since restart sees new massive star on grid → wants to tank the timestep.

And, to allow very large wind perturbations, disabled the `vels don’t match` sanity check in `inject_direct.F90` \(which tries to ensure perturbations are < 5-sigma\).

**Restart test:**

30 Msun star with radiation \+ wind for 10 kyr in a uniform density medium. Both radiation and wind turned on. Randomize ray directions on unit sphere \(`ph_rotRay=T`\) and perturb wind velocity a lot \(`perturb_velocity=T` and `perturb_std_dev=0.2`\). Use only one processor for FLASH worker to remove async ray trace randomness.

* \[x\] Ab initio run compared to restart from chk=0002 agrees to bitwise precision for multiple timesteps in FLASH “dat” file
* \[x\] Restart without `turbsph_txt_RNG_state_0002` proceeds, but warns to both stdout and Flash “log”. Warnings are kinda buried in various Flash init messages though.
* \[x\] Ab initio run with both ionization\+wind, but nproc=4 instead of nproc=1, must use same random number stream as nproc=1 run. Cell-by-cell perturbed wind velocity and the ray-trace rotation matrix must agree at every timestep; all processors' random streams must advance in lockstep. Verified by printing result of `grnd()` at various points in evolution loop.

**Wind regression tests:**

Weaver test, dm/dt=1e-6 Msun/yr and wind v\_term = 2000 km/s in WIM \(n=1 cm^-3, T=1e4 K\). Wind only with `perturb_velocity=T` and `perturb_std_dev=0.2`, radiation is off.

* \[x\] Wind bubble with `perturb_velocity=T`, old \(commit e31fbcc\) vs new scheme has “similarly random” perturbation structure \(similar fluct magnitude, angular variation compare to a non-perturbed wind\)
* \[x\] Wind bubble with `perturb_velocity=T`, 1 vs 4 proc looks nearly identical early on \(not bitwise identical for unknown other reasons beyond scope of this pull request\)
* \[x\] Wind bubble `perturb_velocity=F` , old \(commit e31fbcc\) vs new scheme, same #procs, is bitwise identical

**Other regression tests:**

* \[x\] Sim of gas sphere \+ pre-existing star cluster, ab initio vs. restart runs use same RNG stream, and shows \(by eye\) much better agreement than a run with different RNG stream. Because we use >1 proc, the results are not bitwise identical though.

## Comparison of restarts with consistent vs. inconsistent RNG streams

Ionization and winds are both on, but randomness only occurs in radiation transfer \(`ph_rotRays=T`\).

First, an ab initio simulation from t = 0 to 40.4 kyr.

![](https://bitbucket.org/repo/8XAGbBb/images/570004512-image.png)
Second, a restart from t ~ 16.1 to 40.4 kyr, using the RNG state checkpoint file.

![](https://bitbucket.org/repo/8XAGbBb/images/3924502257-image.png)
Last, a restart from t ~ 16.1 to 40.4 kyr, but reset the RNG state used by ray transfer \(delete the file `turbsph_txt_RNG_state_0001`\).

![](https://bitbucket.org/repo/8XAGbBb/images/3278665960-image.png)
‌

title: Speed up the wind and SN block finding + cell deposit algorithm
description:
Speed up the wind and SN deposit by only doing zone/\{inject sphere\} overlap calculation where needed. For runs with 100s or 1000s of windy stars, this can be a big savings. Also clean up some other stuff.

* Column-major order cell loops for wind, SN inject
* Exact inject block check in wind and GridInject
* minor cleanup in wind,kineticSN cell inject loops
* **wind,kineticSN more efficient cell overlap check**

    also document the overlap routine's tapering behavior


* **Cut cell-overlap MC nsamp to 10^3 for winds, SNe**

    Precision ~ 0.1% is comparable to discretization for 3-cell radius injection region \(approx 100 grid cells\). Want to speed up overlap calculation for runs w/ many stars.


* GridInject documentation

# Regression testing

Three setups:

* “-wind” = Weaver stellar wind in uniform medium
* “-sn” = detonate 30Msun \(ZAMS\) SN in turbulent gas sphere
* “-sn-displace” = same SN, but detonate off center from turbulent gas sphere

The regression test compares the code at three commits:

* “old” = `88ce255` \(no changes\)
* “fixed” = `ab2f4a4` \(more efficient cell overlap check\)
* “fixed-cheap” = `d729ad1` \(reduce overlap\(. . .\) monte carlo #sampling points to 10^3, down from 20^3 \[winds\] and 50^3 \[SN\]\)

author: Aaron Tran

title: Massive star mass tracks SeBa mass loss for all life, not just post-MS
description:
Change massive star mass to track SeBa mass loss for all life, not just post-main-sequence evolution.

During main sequence phase for very massive stars, SeBa models the H-envelope loss, but this is not included in the Kudritzki/Puls/Vink main sequence winds we are using.

* For stars <~ 30 Msun, change is <~1%.
* For stars >~40 Msun, change could be ~10-50% during the last ~0.1-0.5 Myr of ZAMS evolution, before the W-R phase.

Here are a few cases to show the effect:

* 30 Msun: [http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915\_massloss/mass\_evol\_030msun.pdf](http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915_massloss/mass_evol_030msun.pdf)
* 40 Msun: [http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915\_massloss/mass\_evol\_040msun.pdf](http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915_massloss/mass_evol_040msun.pdf)
* 100 Msun: [http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915\_massloss/wind-and-mass\_evol\_100msun.pdf](http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915_massloss/wind-and-mass_evol_100msun.pdf)
* 150 Msun: [http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915\_massloss/wind-and-mass\_evol\_150msun.pdf](http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915_massloss/wind-and-mass_evol_150msun.pdf)

More cases and source code to generate these plots at: [http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915\_massloss/](http://user.astro.columbia.edu/~atran/torch/20200915_massloss/)

The refactor branch is already using the “new” behavior \(by accident, as it turns out…\).

‌

I tried itergrids with my build on Cartesius, same problem. And even when copying your Cartesius build as much as possible, it has the same behavior. So that’s four builds across two systems… I’m guessing I’m doing something wrong in setting up, but I have no idea what.

It turned out not to be setup, but how many workers Flash was assigned. With 1 it works fine, but with any more it breaks. 

I’ve dug around a bit more and have found where it goes wrong, but I still don’t quite understand how it goes wrong. In the get\_grid\_range function \(which gets the number of cells along each axis for a given block\), there is a check for whether the processor you’re on is the processor containing the block you’re querying. It only queries the grid if that check is true. If not, the output values still contain some nonsense values. For some reason, only the values of the first get\_grid\_range call \(corresponding to the 0th processor\) are passed back to amuse, while the values for call to the correct processor are fine. 

This is why the first few shapes in my list were correct, the processors it queries happened to be correct. I have no idea where it gets the nonsense values from, but they seem deterministic. 

I don’t know how MPI aggregates over multiple processors, but fixing that should fix our errors.

So apparently, the root process \(rank 0\) returns to the interface, so all other processes must mpi\_reduce to that process. With my latest commit it appears to be fixed, but I’ll test it a bit more.

All of the grid getters appear to work just fine. However, I’m running into some problems when I try to set grid values, which I really don’t understand. Considering that most grid manipulation is done through Flash, fixing this might not have the highest priority, and the components that I need are now functioning, so maybe we can merge these changes for now.

The two problems I’ve run into:

* If points on the grid are set and then get \(gotten?\), the values are not always the same. This happens more often the more points you query at once \(not at all for 1-100 points, and the fraction increases with points, from ~1 in 27 at 1000, to ~1 in 8 at 10000, to 1 in fewer than 2 for 1.000.000\). I _think_ this is a setter issue, because getting the values of a single block \(4096 cells\) works fine. On the other hand, if I set the values one by one, and get them in bulk, there are still errors, also at 1000 points
* If I try to set grid values through the subgrids yielded by itergrids, I get an error that it has gotten multiple values of the index\_of\_grid parameter. The interface looks the same as that of athena at the revelant point, so I have no idea why it complains here.

See below for minimal examples. In the first, change N to change the number of points to get and set, and comment the two blocks with triple quotes in and out.

```python

                   1.0| units.cm, 1.0 | units.g, 1 | units.s)

hydro = Flash(unit_converter = convert2, number_of_workers=4, redirection='none')

hydro.initialize_code()

hydro.evolve_model( 1. | units.kyr )



N = 1000


# Random points to set/get
Nproc = np.random.randint(0, 4, size=N)
Ngrid = np.zeros(N, dtype=int)
for I in range(N):
    Ngrid[I] = np.random.randint(1, hydro.get_number_of_grids(Nproc[I])+1)

i = np.random.randint(0, 16, size=N)+1
j = np.random.randint(0, 16, size=N)+1
k = np.random.randint(0, 16, size=N)+1


# Random values
rho = np.random.rand(N) | units.g/units.cm**3
rhovx = np.random.rand(N) | units.g/units.cm**3*units.cm/units.s
rhovy = np.random.rand(N) | units.g/units.cm**3*units.cm/units.s
rhovz = np.random.rand(N) | units.g/units.cm**3*units.cm/units.s
energy = np.random.rand(N) | units.erg/units.cm**3


# Get buffer

rhovx_grid = np.zeros(N) | units.g/units.cm**3*units.cm/units.s
rhovy_grid = np.zeros(N) | units.g/units.cm**3*units.cm/units.s
rhovz_grid = np.zeros(N) | units.g/units.cm**3*units.cm/units.s
energy_grid = np.zeros(N) | units.erg/units.cm**3




'''

for I in range(N):
    hydro.set_grid_density(i[I], j[I], k[I], Ngrid[I], Nproc[I], rho[I])
    rho_grid[I] = hydro.get_grid_density(i[I], j[I], k[I], Ngrid[I], Nproc[I])

    rhovx_grid[I], rhovy_grid[I], rhovz_grid[I] = hydro.get_grid_momentum_density(i[I], j[I], k[I], Ngrid[I], Nproc[I])
'''


#'''

hydro.set_grid_density(i, j, k, Ngrid, Nproc, rho)
rho_grid = hydro.get_grid_density(i, j, k, Ngrid, Nproc)

hydro.set_grid_energy_density(i, j, k, Ngrid, Nproc, energy)
energy_grid = hydro.get_grid_energy_density(i, j, k, Ngrid, Nproc)

For grid setters:

```

from amuse.datamodel import Particle

convert2 = generic_unit_converter.ConvertBetweenGenericAndSiUnits(
                   1.0| units.cm, 1.0 | units.g, 1 | units.s)

hydro = Flash(unit_converter = convert2, number_of_workers=4, redirection='none')

for subgrid in hydro.itergrids():
    subgrid.rho = 1. | units.g/units.cm**3
```

I made a couple more changes, mostly formatting, and moved queries for the current processor outside some do loops.

‌

I agree that manipulation of the grid from outside of FLASH is not necessarily a priority right now, but it would be good to pin down this issue more in case anyone needs to alter a run in this way in the future. Can you qualify what you mean by values that have been set/gotten are not the same sometimes? How different are the values, a few % or entirely different?   
Ultimately, I think merging into master and refactor is good to go as of now, but let’s also make an issue on the issues list about this set/get behavior.

After barking up some wrong trees for the 1st bug \(haven’t touched the 2nd bug\), I think the set\(..\)/get\(…\) problem is due to collisions in the grid cell sampling, lines 22 to 30 of the minimal working example. After editing the minimal working example to sample cells without replacement, that seems to get rid of the errors.

With D = 36,864 = 16\*16\*16\*9 cells \(1 parent blocks, 8 leaf blocks\), we expect:

* n=1,000 <collisions> = 12 \(1 in 80\)
* n=10,000 <collisions> = 1241 \(1 in 8\)
* n=100,000 <collisions> = 65582 \(more than 1 in 2\)

where <collisions> is calculated following [http://matt.might.net/articles/counting-hash-collisions/](http://matt.might.net/articles/counting-hash-collisions/) . The predicted #collisions matches Martijn’s report quite well at n=10,000. For n=1,000, the predicted #collisions seems a bit low since 1 in 27 translates to ~37 collisions \(in my tests I see ~10-20; have not checked the probability\(#collisions\) distribution width for n=1000\).

That makes sense, seems I made a mistake in my tests \(again\)… So it seems like there’s no error?

I can look at the other error again later, maybe early next week, for now I need to step away and clear my head \(lest I make another error like that :sweat_smile: \).

> So it seems like there’s no error?

I think so :slight_smile: thanks for the careful cleanup and testing of all these interface methods. I also probably cannot look at the 2nd error in the near future, but concur w/ both your guys' assessment that it’s OK to merge.  Since you guys are both actively developing / working quite a bit more \(not sure if there are any other plans for branch cleanup\), would one of you be able to take the lead on that?

I think it should be fine to merge now! I’d like to get these changes into my own branch so I can start my runs. I’m no git expert, so I’m not sure how to get these changes into refactor. Can I look at Sean for this?

Also, a final note for posterity: setting grid quantities through the subgrids yielded by itergrids does not work \(at least with these changes\), but directly using set\_grid\_\{quantity\} does. You do have to figure out what grid and processor you need to access, but get\_index\_of\_position also gives those for a certain physical position. Also, there are always as many processors as Flash workers N \(running from 0 to N-1\), and the function get\_number\_of\_grids gets the number of grids on a processor M, running from 1 to M.

I believe once this pull request is complete and it is all merged into master, then we can merge master in to refactor and that should bring over all the changes you’ve made here. I don’t see why there would be any issues with that process, especially since only interface.F90 and interface.py are being modified.

title: [WIP] Slight Reorganization for to Improve Tycho's End User Experience
description:
I have slightly reorganized the main repository \(based off of the `refactor` branch which is now `main`\) to bring Torch in line with an end-user’s expectations for a Python package.

I have a basic `setup.py` created, moved the core Torch functionalities into the `src/torch` folder so that folks can do a `pip install -e .` and get the package up and running.

I have summarized changes in the new `README.md` file and updated the `CONTRIBUTION.md` to link to the correct AMUSE Style Sheet.

~ Joe G.

* `src/torch/legacy/bridge_multiples.py` , what do you think of placing closer to top level, since from a user’s perspective it is more interchangeable with `torch_user.py` ?
* Do I understand right that, setuptools \+ `pip install -e` serves as a replacement for the use of explicit `export PYTHONPATH=...` ?

    * I am concerned that it adds another layer of indirection \(via symlinking through `site-packages`\), which has to be deciphered when tracing which packages and executables are being used in a given Torch run.  So I guess I am not totally sold on preferring this style of usage, as opposed to trying to keep the files / path resolution more “shallow” / explicit.  But maybe that is not too much overhead, or the same amount of overhead as keeping track of one’s `PATH` and `PYTHONPATH` variables already, and I’m happy to be convinced otherwise – part of my hesitation is also simple ignorance of how to develop within a setuptools / pip paradigm.
    * If we always call `pip install -e .` for Torch, should we advise the user to also use `pip install -e .` in the AMUSE repo as well?  \(i personally have not been using any package management with AMUSE, but always doing manual configure / make locally!\)

The idea there being that users can call `torch.legacy.bridge_multiples` with an import function if they want anything from that massive script file.
2. So `PYTHONPATH` has been unofficially deprecated by the wider python community in favor of using `pip` to install small packages within environments \(either `virtenv` or `conda env`\). Currently, AMUSE suggests using the `pip` method of installation for the development branch rather than setting PYTHONPATHs like we use to do in the Python 2.7 days. This brings us in line with what current software packages do.

‌

There is no harm in doing this, though if you have multiple copies of torch floating around, you really should be using virtenv or conda environments to handle this and ensure you can make quick jumps around. The `-e` tag says that all of the python eggs are built referencing the `$TORCH_DIR/src`.

title: Add "-d" or "--dev" flag for setup_simulation.sh
description:
## Synopsis

Automatically copy all the Torch python modules into your run directory. Closes issue [#48](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/48/setup_sim-d-dev-flag-for-python-module) brought up at Torch CCA workshop \([https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/48/setup\_sim-d-dev-flag-for-python-module](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/48/setup_sim-d-dev-flag-for-python-module)\).

Super simple / meant to be user customizable! I gave up on trying to be clever with command line options because of [YAGNI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/You_aren%27t_gonna_need_it), so the code looks for exactly one command-line arg “-d” or “--dev”, and in all other situations reverts to default behavior. No attempt made to copy / “freeze” AMUSE-FLASH interface code that might be altered, for now.

A downside is that the extra modules clutter up the run directory. This situation will be improved by Joe’s reorganization \([https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/pull-requests/28](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/pull-requests/28)\), which will let us copy `src/torch/*.py` into a run directory as a stand-alone local package, so when Joe’s reorganization is merged into main we should update this script as well.

## Testing

Because this commit only affects the Python module import and does not alter / depend on compiled code, the change was tested just on my own local cluster by the following procedure:

1. Call `setup_simulation.sh` without `-d` flag, behaves same as before and no python modules copied over.
2. Call `setup_simulation.sh` with `-d` and `--dev` flag, all python modules copied into top level of run directory.
3. Run a torch simulation with the “local” modules in the run directory, each file manually hand-edited to declare that it’s the “local” run-directory version rather than the `$TORCH_DIR` version. It worked, STDOUT below:

```
using local import param
using local import ionizingflux.py
using local import torch stdout
using local import torch_se
using local import imf_sample
using local import torch_sf
using local import torchstate
using local import torch mainloop
[torch 10-06-2022 12:42:49.847] Num hydro workers: 2
[torch 10-06-2022 12:42:49.847] Num grav workers: 1
[torch 10-06-2022 12:42:49.847] AMUSE overwrite: True
. . .
```

## Checklist for merging

* \[n/a, see above\] code successfully builds and run on Snellius, and ideally >=1 other cluster too
* \[Discussed at AMUSE Workshop\] you have announced your changes at a torch user's group meeting
* \[ x \] at least 1 other person reviewed and approved your PR

‌

title: Fuv field fix
description:
A correction to FUV flux calculation, based on the projected area of cubic cells. This removes a 50% overestimate of the flux. Also included are a number of fixes to the grid interface.

I tested this on a computer in Leiden and on Snellius. Attached you can find a minimal script that demonstrates how the flux depends on the distance to a massive star. With default Torch, this should be biased, with this change, it’s practically gone. This requires a normal Torch setup, with the example cube file \(cube128\), default cool.dat file, and an edited flash.par file \(also attached; major change is to reduce the dust cross section to a negligible value to showcase 1/r^2 behavior\).

title: Fix of initial timestep limiters after feedback star forms
description:
## Synopsis

There are 3 things this pull request addresses:

1. The timestep limiters in RadTrans\_computeDt.F90

    1. `dt_wind` was originally computed assuming the winds would heat gas to 2e7 K. This was a "magic number" in the code. However, this defeats the purpose of mass-loading to reduce gas temperatures and the timestep. So I've replaced the magic number with the user parameter of `wind_target_temp` which is the temperature mass loaded winds will heat the gas to. `dt_wind` also used the actual wind velocity of the star rather than the mass-loaded velocity, which I've also fixed.
    2. `dt_min_local` uses another magic number, 2e5 K, which is the temperature radiation is estimated to heat the gas up to. Rather than have this be a hidden number, I've changed it to be a user parameter `rt_dt_temp` with a default value of 1e5 K.
    3. `dt_mom` limits the timestep based on how much velocity is expected to be added to the grid cells due to the new stars radiation pressure. The previous calculation was dt = 0.3 \(mean free path\)/dv\_radpressure. However, this doesn’t make sense. It should be dt=0.3 dx/dv\_radpress, with dx being the cell width. I’ve also made the 0.3 a user parameter `cfl_radPressure` with a default value of 0.3.
    
2. The updating of the UV Flux field variable `UVFL_VAR` in `pt_solveZone.F90`

    1. Martijn pointed out that the updated of the UV flux field variable is wrong. It uses the ionizing photon rate of a cell to calculate the flux, instead of simply adding the flux per absorbed photon of the ray. Because the rate is updated and added to the rate in the cell before the flux uses it, the flux can sometimes double count rays. To fix this, I simply replace the flux calculation using the rate with one using the number of absorbed photons: Flux = N\_absorbed \* E\_avg / \(dt\*Area\).
    2. Here is an explanation of the flawed logic of the previous code, written by Martijn Wilhelm:
    

> In `pt_solveZone.F90` , line 84, it gets the current ionization rate \(from previous rays in this step\), then in line 160, the current ray's ionization is added, and in line 172 it's again written away \(all with the `PHIO_VAR` variable, so that looks fine\). Then in line 192, the radiation flux is calculated from the total ionization rate, so that should be the total of all rays in that step \(up to and including the current ray\). But in line 196-198, it's added to the previous ray's `UVFL_VAR`. For the second ray, `UVFL_VAR` already contains ray 1, but now it adds both ray 1 and 2.

1. The lack of a timestep limiter of the velocity imparted by radiation pressure of rays

    1. There is currently no timestep limiter that limits the acceleration of gas in a cell due to each passing ray’s momentum transfer due to radiation pressure. However, this isn’t an issue when winds are on as the wind timestep will always be more restrictive than the radiation pressure timestep. This is unlikely to occur in most Torch simulations, but if a user ever decides to turn winds off and keep radiation pressure on, I’ve added a warning of this problem in `torch_user.py` next to the winds on/off flag.
    

‌

## Tests

1. Fix 1: RadTrans\_computeDt.F90

    1. The first test performed for the changes to RadTrans\_computeDt.F90 was of a single 100Msun feedback star initialized in the center of a cloud in a number density of n=10^4. The timesteps calculated proved stable for the calculation, and didn’t produce a crash.
    2. The second test was just starting the same 10^6 Msun simulation, right before star formation commences. Over 20 steps of star formation happens, and at no time did the new timesteps cause the simulation to become unstable.
    
2. Fix 2: pt\_solveZone.F90

    1. This change was a single line of code, and the only thing changed was a physical equation. Therefore, no tests of the functionality of the code is required. This fix just requires a check by the reviewer that the new equation is correct, and the logic of the equation fixed the issue.
    
3. Fix 3: torch\_user.py

    1. This change was simply a comment, no tests necessary.
    

‌

* \[x\] code successfully builds and run on Snellius, and ideally >=1 other cluster too

    * compiles and runs on Snellius
    
* \[x\] you have announced your changes at a torch user's group meeting

    * announced at meeting Thursday Jan 26th
    
* \[ \] at least 1 other person reviewed and approved your PR

    * Martijn will be the other reviewer and will grant approval
    

‌

title: fix small mass agglomeration algorithm
description:
The small mass agglomeration algorithm acts upon each sink stellar mass list. When a sink forms, all stellar masses below `m_small` will be agglomerated into stars of at most mass `m_small`. The mass list is then shuffled and used as usual.  
  
The previous version of the small mass algorithm made use of the `numpy.delete()` method for each particle agglomeration which forces the acted upon array to be fully copied; a very slow operation. Since this operation is repeated for **each** star of mass < m\_small, the algorithm takes prohibitively long times for very large sink mass lists: ~0.66 years for a 1e7Msun sink.   
  
I’ve reworked the algorithm to operate on a single array in-place, cleaning up the post-agglomeration array **once** rather than for each agglomerated particle. Operating on a 1e7Msun sink mass list now takes ~20 seconds.  
  
Brooke is a co-reviewer as she currently uses the mass agglomeration algo.  
  
This fix also allows for user-control of the `m_small` threshold at the `torch_user.py` level. This branch is consistent with `main` beyond this fix and has been successfully tested on Snellius.

title: Stars ang momentum
description:
**Description of changes:**

The goal of the changes in this branch is to ensure that star particles have an angular momentum direction that can eventually be used in the jets branch.

Both a set\_particle\_ang\_mom\(\) function and a get\_sink\_ang\_mom\(\) function already existed in the interface.py and interface.F90 files. The former was set up to handle both star and sink particle types while the latter function could only handle sinks. I could not find anywhere that either function was used in the current version of Torch and so updated the get\_sink\_ang\_mom\(\) function to be a more general get\_particle\_ang\_mom\(\) function that can handle both types of particle in the same way that the other interface functions do.

I then modified torch\_sf.py to use these functions to set the angular momentum of newly formed star particles based on the angular momentum of the sink particle from which the star formed \(the angular momentum of which is already set\). To avoid fully accounting for the complicated transfer and dissipation of angular momentum in the star formation process, we assign the star particle a normalized, unit vector with the direction of the sink particle’s angular momentum vector but with a magnitude of 1. An additional random perturbation is added to the angular momentum direction using np.random.normal.  The standard deviation of the random variations can be updated but is currently set to 0.1.

**Description of tests:**

I have performed a somewhat low resolution turbulent sphere test \(similar to that in the quickstart guide, I believe\). I am able to run with this setup and also to restart the run. The masses and angular momentum values of the star particles are constant for this run.

I also tested restarting within this branch one of Sean’s runs that was started in main.  I similarly tested restarting one of my stars-ang-momentum branch runs in main.  Both runs restart.  I hope this indicates backward compatibility for runs already started in main.  Please let me know if you would like to see additional tests of this.

_**Update as of July 11:**_

I have merged in recent changes from the main branch in preparation for this pull request. I believe these commits only include changes to the quickstart guide and an update to an integer type.  As such, I don’t expect these commits to affect the angular momentum handling. 

Using the same turbulent sphere test above, the branch still compiles fine. The turbulent sphere setup still runs and the stellar mass and angular momentum remain constant as expected.

I also ran a test with a single star in a box. The code compiled and ran fine. When I did not set the star particle angular momentum in torch\_user.py, each component was initialized to zero.  I also confirmed that setting the star particle’s angular momentum in torch\_user.py updates the angular momentum properties as expected. The lines needed to make this update are not currently included in the torch\_user.py file.

Please let me know if there are additional tests that you think would be beneficial for me to perform on this branch. 

**Checklist for merging:**

* \[ \] code successfully builds and run on Snellius, and ideally >=1 other cluster too

    * Able to build and run on rusty.  I don’t have access to Snellius so it would be great if someone can check there.
    
* \[X\] you have announced your changes at a torch user's group meeting

    * Mentioned at the June 26 and July 10 meetings.  Will address it again on July 17.
    
* \[ \] at least 1 other person reviewed and approved your PR

‌
author: Sabrina Appel

comments.content:
_**Update July 18, 2023:**_

Added a brief subsection to section 10 of the manual describing the handling of angular momentum.

Also, added lines to torch\_user.py to set the angular momentum of the star when starting with a single star on the grid. This same code can be extrapolated for starting with several stars.

I’m a bit worried about how the angular momentum is defined right now; you mention that you’re only interested in the direction of the vector, but it currently has units, so that may be confusing in the future. If you mean to keep just the direction, defining it as unitless is probably clearest.  
Also, about conservation, I’m worried that the small variations can induce spurious angular momentum. If a newly formed star inherits all mass, and then produces jets, the angular momentum that the jets introduce is misaligned with that of the gas that had formed the sink. That may average out over small stars, but with rare massive stars there may be issues.   
I guess that requires fully conserving angular momentum, which I get is tricky, also because of how the mass subtraction from the sink and formation of stars are separated…

To summarize our conversation during the User’s Group Meeting today:

Yes!  Good point about the units.  I’ll go in and remove the units on the angular momentum property to make it clearer that the angular momentum is a normalized vector.

Unfortunately, we aren’t really tracking angular momentum conservation at all and there isn’t a simple way to do that since we aren’t resolving the accretion process, disk, etc and we aren’t tracking how angular momentum is dispersed into the gas. We are getting rid of a lot of angular momentum when we form the stars, so there shouldn’t be an issue with a sudden increase in angular momentum, though we are obviously losing a lot of angular momentum.  And if there is an occasional massive star with a misaligned jet, I think this can be considered an approximation of a particularly chaotic or messy accretion process.  All of that said, I definitely think this is something to keep in mind going forward and might be worth revisiting now and then as we add features to the code.

title: [To review] Fixes to the wind injection code
description:
**Overview:**

This branch was originally created to update the wind injection routine such that inject\_direct does not loop over every star \(including non-wind stars\) within Particles\_wind.F90. It turns out that Particles\_wind.F90 already skips the non-wind stars through clever indexing so this pull request is less urgent. However, I have used this branch to add explicit checks as a back up before calling inject\_direct and to update a few other small aspects of the winds routine. The main changes are:

* I have added an explicit check at the start of inject\_direct.F90 to exit the routine if nothing is being injected.
* I have added an explicit check right before the inject\_direct call in Particles\_wind.F90 to skip the inject\_direct call if dmdt = 0.
* I have added Timers calls to Particles\_wind.F90 that can be used to time the wind injection routine .

    * These calls are wrapped in pre-processor flags and so can easily be removed.
    * The Timers calls are also dependent on the Timers routine working properly on the exit of the FLASH routine, which is a separate ongoing concern.
    
* I have updated the inject\_direct call and subroutine to remove the starMass argument. The star’s mass is not easily available at the time that inject\_direct is called without an additional MPI\_AllGather call and is not needed in inject\_direct aside from a non-critical print statement. In the previous version of the winds routine, the starMass argument was passed the total injection mass. This duplicates the injectMassIn argument and is also then misleading in the print statements within inject\_direct.F90.

    * I have updated the subroutine definition in inject\_direct.F90 and removed all uses of the starMass variable later in the code.
    * I have updated Particles\_wind.F90 and sink/Particles\_sinkWind.F90 to include the correct arguments.
    
* I have also updated pt\_windInterface.F90 to reflect the current arguments of inject\_direct.

‌

**Tests:**

I have tested this branch on a standard 10^3 turbulent sphere set up with min/max refinement of 2/4. This run produced 335 stars, including 4 feedback stars. Let me know if there are other tests we should run.

‌

title: Binaries
description:
The main new feature of this branch is the implementation of **primordial binaries** in Torch. A few additional changes to Torch are also included in the pull request:

* Redirection of PeTar output to new petar\_worker.out and petar\_worker.err files
* Change to default PeTar parameters to ensure that collisional N-body dynamics are followed properly
* Routine identifying merged stars every timestep, and saving the properties of the stars at the merger
* Routine saving the properties of the stars escaping the box, including their exact escape time

‌

**Primordial binaries**

Usage:

* Set `p['binaries']=True` in `torch_user.py`

The primordial binaries implemented in this branch are based on observations compiled by Moe & di Stefano \(2017\) for solar-type and early-type stars, and on the observations by Winters et al. \(2019, as corrected by Offner et al. 2022\) for low-mass stars. The sampling routine for binaries is stored in `primordial_binaries.py`, which can be readily modified to allow for other prescriptions. The values in the present version however reflect the mass-dependent binary fraction observed for main sequence stars, as well as the mass-dependent period distribution, and the mass- and period-dependent mass ratio and eccentricity distributions. Those distributions are shown in the plots below.

_Binary fraction_

![](https://bitbucket.org/repo/8XAGbBb/images/4054309451-multiplicity_fraction.png){: data-layout='center' }
‌

_Orbital period distributions \(colours match the colours from the binary fraction plot\)_

![](https://bitbucket.org/repo/8XAGbBb/images/2738617427-periods.png){: data-layout='center' }
‌

_Mass ratio distributions \(same colours as above\)_

![](https://bitbucket.org/repo/8XAGbBb/images/25366079-mass_ratios.png){: data-layout='center' }
‌

_Eccentricity distribution_

![](https://bitbucket.org/repo/8XAGbBb/images/844233898-eccentricities.png){: data-layout='center' }
Although only the single stars and primaries are formally drawn from the IMF, the stellar mass function \(including the companions\) remains a very close match to the Kroupa IMF \(see below\).

![](https://bitbucket.org/repo/8XAGbBb/images/3531834821-imf_with_binaries.png){: data-layout='center' }
‌

**Updated PeTar parameters**

PeTar was designed for pure N-body simulations, and would usually set a small changeover radius \(to minimize the cost of direct N-body calculations\) in conjunction with a short timestep. To use PeTar in Torch and ensure that collisional dynamics are followed, the changeover radius r\_out between direct N-body and gravity tree approaches must be set along with a maximum simulation timestep. The criterion suggested on the Petar github is that binary orbits should be resolved by several 10’s of direct N-body code timesteps. In the plot below, we show combinations of r\_out and maximum timesteps where a binary’s orbit is resolved by 10 or 100 timesteps at r\_in and r\_out. The recommended usage is to pick a pair of r\_out and maximum timestep along or below the red line, which indicates that a binary’s orbit is resolved by at least 100 timesteps for separations as short as r\_in. The black star a very conservative choice \(r\_out = 0.03 pc, dt\_max = 125 yr\) while the default is r\_out=0.003 pc, dt\_max = 125 yr. Note that this timestep criterion can be set by the user, and is only enforced once stars have formed.

Usage:

* Set `p['r_out']` in `torch_user.py`

title: VorAMR
description:
This pull request includes VorAMR, a tool built by Sean Lewis to convert Voronoi mesh simulation data, i.e. Arepo, into Torch initial conditions. The description and usage of VorAMR is in the quickstart. A proof of concept run with VorAMR was done by Sean, and a paper describing VorAMR and the test run is submitted.

The capability to derefine the grid outside a rectangular region is also included in this pull request. This was implemented and tested by Brooke Polak. Note that neighboring cells can only have a difference of one level of refinement, so keep this in mind when selecting your region of interest and de-refinement level. Here is this feature in use:

![](https://bitbucket.org/repo/8XAGbBb/images/4039077404-refine.gif)
To use this feature, set the following parameters in flash.par:

```
# ===================================================
# Derefinement outside rectangular region of interest
# ===================================================
use_deref         = .true.
deref_lref        = 2  # level to derefine to
# EXAMPLE: derefine to level deref_lref outside center 5pc square
deref_xl          = -1.543e+19  # 5pc
deref_xr          =  1.543e+19 
deref_yl          = -1.543e+19
deref_yr          =  1.543e+19
deref_zl          = -1.543e+19
deref_zr          =  1.543e+19
```

title: [To review] restart-with-seba merge into main
description:
Supports restarts with evolved stars, without re-running stellar evolution. FLASH particles now contain all the information to restart a star in its current evolved state. This capability is also designed to be used with interacting binaries, for which a star’s specific history is important.

The change was tested for restart from a previous Torch checkpoint \(without the saved stellar properties\), and for restart from a checkpoint with the stellar properties.

Added features:

* Addition of the particle properties relative mass, relative age, core mass, CO core mass, radius, and stellar type. Those properties are the minimum set of properties required to restart a set of stars in SeBa from their current state.
* We are now following the AMUSE convention of `worker.evolve_model` for stellar evolution. This avoids looping through the full star list at each stellar evolution step \(see below for possible conflict with other branches\).
* The initial radius is set using a mass-radius relation rather than setting it to a \(very large\) collisional radius – the previous value was 100 au. Each stellar evolution step also updates the radius.
* Stars with separations less than the sum of their radii are merged with SeBa. This check is done at every timestep.
* Update quickstart with installation instructions for Stampede3 and added references to PeTar and SeBa.

General usage:

* Requires an AMUSE version from Feb 28, 2025 or later
* To make use of the stellar merger feature, set the `remove_merged` flag to `True` in torch\_user.py
* No change in `torch_user.py` necessary to restart correctly with evolved stars
* Compatible with restarts from previous versions

Potential conflicts:

* Setting the radius to a physical rather than collisional radius overwrites the radius chosen for `multiples`, which was 100 au. Although this is not incompatible with use with multiples, it does mean that multiples won’t pick up \(most of the\) close encounters. This is likely not the dominant effect in simulations without primordial binaries, so the main takeaway would be that I don’t recommend using this if you’re running with `multiples` and primordial binaries \(which should not be not by anyone at this point\).
* If restarting from a simulation that used the previous stellar evolution step, the stellar properties before the first stellar evolution step will be wrong as we are no longer running a SE step during the restart. This should not have important effects, as the stellar mass is incorrect and the SE step takes place before feedback is injected.
* At least one other branch \(the PeTar branch\) only evolves massive stars. This should not create any additional conflicts compared to the main branch, since the SE step is overwritten by this pull request, but we should be aware of it.

Checklist for merging:

* \[x\] code successfully builds and run on Snellius, and ideally >=1 other cluster too
* \[x\] you have announced your changes at a torch user's group meeting
* \[x\] at least 1 other person reviewed and approved your PR

Tests for review \(**make sure to do those test with the latest version of AMUSE**\):

* \[x\] quickstart
* \[x\] restart from a previous run with stars
* \[x\] double-check the stellar masses at restart

title: Interacting binaries
description:
The main new feature of this branch is the implementation of **feedback from interacting binaries** in Torch. A few additional changes to Torch are also included in the pull request:

* Change of default `'massloss_method'` in `torch_user.py` to to the new method `'seba_puls'`, which uses the wind mass loss rates from SeBa and the velocities from Puls\+ 2000 \(which were already the default\). Note that the SeBa wind mass loss rates have been updated since the last SeBa methods papers, and are now the Vink\+2000, 2001 mass loss rates, with a correction factor of 1/3 to account for clumping.
* The Eddington factor \(Gamma\) for the stars' effective escape velocities is now capped at 0.8. This has no effect for main sequence stars but ensures that stars that are out of thermodynamical equilibrium \(mostly binary interaction products and evolved stars\) don’t get assigned imaginary escape velocities.
* The minimum feedback mass now depends on the initial mass of a star, rather than its present-day mass.
* FLASH’s `DEBUG_OVERHEAT` is now off by default, to avoid crashes at high temperatures \(which otherwise happen after mass loss episodes during binary interaction\). **If preferred, this could just be set to a value higher than 1e9 K.**

**Interacting binaries**

Usage:

* Set `p['binary_evolution']=True` in `torch_user.py`

This enables binary evolution for systems in which at least one star has an initial mass above the feedback limit. The default parameters use wind velocities for all ejecta from the binaries.

**Tests**

This branch is currently used on Trillium, and was used on Stampede3, Graham, and Niagara.

author: Brooke Polak

comments.content:
The code successfully compiles on Trillium. I also ran the test successfully on that machine. I have two comments regarding the code itself \(see below\). I don’t think either _needs_ to be addressed before merging with main, as they do not introduce new errors, but I think addressing the first one would be good.  
  
1\. Supernovae are identified by looking for new remnants. This however does not account for stars that would collapse directly into a black hole, which are a possible outcome in SeBa. In those cases, the injected mass will correspond to the wind mass loss over the timestep; since the supernova routine is energy-based, injecting large amounts of energy for small masses will result in either unphysically fast or unphysically hot ejecta. This issue was already present in the previous routine, but it might be a good time to address it.  
A potential fix would be to require both the stellar type to be between 13 and 15 for the first _and_ the mass loss to be larger than the wind mass for the star. I’d suggest picking a lower limit on the change in mass that we’d consider reasonable for a supernova, and to only trigger the SN routine if the mass loss is above that limit.   
The maximum wind mass loss rates we’d expect are in the ballpark of 1e-4 MSun/yr, even for LBV stars. To be safe, I’d suggest triggering the SN routine if  
**inj\_mass > 1e-3 MSun/yr \* dt**  
to account for direct collapse cases. Let me know what you think!  
  
2\. If/when we add a mass mask for the feedback/SN routine, the mask mass should be based on the star’s initial mass, not its present-day mass. I have something like that implemented in the interacting binaries branch, where I only loop through stars with s.initial\_mass >= min\_feedback\_mass – I’d suggest something similar here :slight_smile:
This pull request was partially tested with test\_sn.py, now deprecated by removing the unit\_test parameter to stellar evolution in torch\_se.py.

title: New amuse flash build system
description:
This updates the AMUSE bindings for Flash to use the new AMUSE build system, so that TORCH can be used with AMUSE v2025.5 and later.

The bindings themselves \(in interface.py and the .F90 files\) are still exactly the same, but the build system is different.

install.sh has been updated to no longer patch AMUSE in place, because with the new build system bindings can be built independently as long as an amuse-framework package is installed.

To try this from scratch:

* create a Conda environment with the required dependencies \(note that we’re not using nose any more\)
* install AMUSE 2025.9.0 according to the instructions at [http://www.amusecode.org](http://www.amusecode.org) \(only amuse-framework and the codes used by TORCH are needed\)
* set TORCH\_DIR and FLASH\_DIR
* run install.sh to copy the TORCH Fortran files into FLASH and link FLASH into the AMUSE bindings dir
* make -C src/amuse/torch\_amuse\_flash develop-torch-amuse-flash
* set PYTHONPATH
* update your scripts to import Flash from torch\_amuse\_flash rather than amuse.community.flash

You should then be able to run TORCH as before. I’m getting an image that looks a lot like Figure 2 in the Quick Start now. \(I was before actually, I just copy-pasted from the Quick Start without thinking and therefore plotted the state at t=0, which explains why it showed the initial conditions :sweat_smile: .\)

Note that this does not include the voramrLite bindings, which I had made but which are obsolete, so I’ve removed them again. This PR leaves voramrLite untouched.

I’ll go put the above into the Quick Start and make a separate PR for that, and we can discuss all of it in the workshop in the week of the 20th.

author: LourensVeen

title: Symlink torch files into flash
description:
Torch is in part a patch on top of FLASH, in the form of a bunch of Fortran files that are either modified versions of the corresponding FLASH files, or new additions. Currently, rsync is used to copy these files on top of the existing files in FLASH, after which it can be built.

As a result, every time you save your file, you need to run `./install.sh` to copy your modified file from Torch into FLASH, or alternatively you need to edit things on the FLASH side and then figure out how to get that back into the git-controlled Torch. This is not so ergonomic.

This PR adds a `./relink.sh` script which runs through the Torch source files, and for each one finds the corresponding FLASH file, if it exists saves a backup copy, and then replaces it with a symbolic link to the file in the Torch tree. It then checks the FLASH tree for any dead links, and if it finds any replaces them with the saved original FLASH file.

As a result, you can now edit Fortran source files in the Torch tree, save, and recompile, without any additional steps. If you add or remove a file \(perhaps by switching branches\), then you need to run `./relink.sh` to update the links. This should make working with Torch a bit more ergonomic.

title: Fix for incorrect relative ages after restart
description:
As described in [issue #73](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/73/incorrect-seba-relative-ages-after-restart), at each restart, the `relative_age` property is increased by the current simulation time. This leads to evolutionary ages for the stars that are much larger than they should be. This pull request fixes this issue by making two changes to the Torch python source code:  
1\. Rather than using `se.evolve_model(time)`, we now use `se.evolve_model(se_time)`, where the stellar evolution time `se_time` is either the time since the onset of star formation \(if there was no star formation prior to the current restart\) or the time since the restart. The stellar evolution time is calculated from   
`se_time = time - max(se_restart_time, min(hydro.get_particle_creation_time(state.stars.tag)))`  
2\. To enable that change, the parameter `se_restart_time`is set to the current simulation time when the simulation is initialized.

Tests:  
1\. Run the cube test problem until there has been at least two episodes of star formation.  
2\. Restart the test problem from the above point, and run until there have been another few episodes of star formation.  
3\.. Check that the relative age and the age inferred from the particles' creation time match. Small differences are ok \(see the discussion in [issue #73](https://bitbucket.org/torch-sf/torch/issues/73/incorrect-seba-relative-ages-after-restart)\) but the critical thing to look out for is that there should be no offset associated with the restart. Plotting relative age vs age from creation time is a good sanity check.

    age = stars.age.value_in(units.Myr)
    perc_diff = abs(rel_age-age)/((age+rel_age)/2)

    plt.scatter([count]*len(rel_age), perc_diff, c=color, marker='.')
    count+=1

plt.xlabel('Snapshot')
plt.ylabel('% difference, relative age and age')
plt.savefig('ageplot.png')
```

‌
Note that the y axis is percent in decimal form, i.e., not multiplied by 100

Brief note: the test with the non-quickstart initial conditions has a much smaller initial offset between the relative age and age \(before falling to a very small difference like the cube128 setup\). This makes me think that there may be some resolution dependence in this initial offset.

title: Vettam
description:
This pull requests merges in the VETTAM radiative transfer module into Torch. Documentation for VETTAM is on the Torch readthedocs website. To use VETTAM, run the setup\_simulation script to get the necessary files and copy over the flash.par.vettam parameter file.

VETTAM has been tested with both a single star in a box and in star cluster simulations. The plot of the results for the r-type and d-type tests are attached below. The code for this test has been added to a tests folder to serve as a standard torch radiation test. Following the example in run.sh, this should create a plot file called stromgren-test.png that shows the r-type and d-type expansions against the analytical solution. 

Note that the low-resolution quickstart test simulation with VETTAM differs significantly from FERVENT. This is because at low resolution, the source region in VETTAM is unphysically large while FERVENT source regions remain point sources. This is important to keep in mind with future VETTAM runs – resolution must be high enough that the VETTAM source region of 0.5-1dx approaches the Stromgren radius.

For reference, I’ve attached the tarball diff\_vettam\_torch.tar.gz. This contains nested diff files showing all the changes I’ve made to the vanilla VETTAM I received from Shyam. Also included is the script used to generate the output files. These changes were made to: integrate with Torch, address bugs, and accommodate changes from FLASH version 4.2 to 4.6.2.

Checklist for merging:

* :white_check_mark: code successfully builds and run on Snellius, and ideally >=1 other cluster too
* :white_check_mark: you have announced your changes at a torch user's group meeting
* :white_check_mark: at least 1 other person reviewed and approved your PR

‌
author: Brooke Polak

comments.content: Preventing stars from forming in warm gas is unphysical in our model, because sink particles already determined that the gas is ready to form stars. This was only included to cap the velocities stars are formed with. In vettam because the source regions are larger, this edge case is triggered more than in FERVENT. Therefore, I’ve changed the code to cap the stars velocities to the sound speed at T=100K.

This file was added because the line 

```
!grid_changed = 0
```

needed to be commented out. VETTAM needs to know whether the grid refinement changed to reallocate matrices memory. Because radiation solve comes after hydro and refinement, resetting this to zero here makes VETTAM think the grid didn’t change so we get a seg fault. Don’t worry, this is reset to 0 anyways before the refinement routine is called. 

‌
These lines are removed because they are already in the Grid\_markRefineDerefine.F90 file in the Cube simulation directory and are not needed here.
These should be removed
Remove parameters use for fervent and document what is fervent and what is vettam
These should be removed
These should technically be all caps to follow flash standard
remove this file
remove this file
remove this file

This file should go to utils

Also add a description of what this script is doing at the top
The compiler flags should be included for properties that are only relevant for fervent. Check if this is the case for any of these and potentially add a flag for vettam.

I’m in favor of keeping all amuse getters/setters compiled for versatility on the amuse side. These values are all used by SeBa, so if there is ever a time we run torch with seba and no radiation, say in a future test case, this could cause an issue.

There are duplicate `plot_var_15` and `plot_var_16`. This was resolved in `flash.par.turbsph_standard` by the following:  

```
plot_var_15 = "uvfl"
plot_var_16 = "fufl"
plot_var_17 = "auvf"
plot_var_18 = "afuf"
...
```

We need to make the same change in `flash.par.vettam` to ensure those values are all properly stored.

In the tests you showed in the slack channel with Shyam, it looked like 0.5 was also OK for convergence. Since you recommend using the smallest possible source size \(especially at low resolution\), would it make sense to have the default be 0.5? Or at least have the note say >= 0.5 rather than >= 1?
This is the last remaining instance of `ifdef FERVENT`, and it’s already commented out. I would suggest removing lines 6807 and 6808, since the comment is no longer relevant.

The code compiles and runs on Trillium. I’ve suggested a few minor changes to `flash.par` and one to `interface.F90`. I’ve also updated the README to include Shyam’s contributions \(in the “Torch also builds on software by…“ section\). Once you’ve addressed my comments, I’ll be happy to approve the pull request.

‌
I just deleted them because VETTAM doesn’t update these quantities.
I changed the note and not the default, because in the test you can see that in the sigma=0.5dx case does start to dip slightly below the analytical value while 1dx stays on the line.
Done!
